behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: The TSP heuristic is pure computation on local data with no I/O, external APIs, or LLM dependencies. A real failure would be an algorithmic bug (incorrect distance calculation, wrong greedy selection, etc.) that produces a suboptimal route. This can be caught by providing mocked geocoding coordinates and verifying the output route length against known optimal solutions for benchmark instances. The test boundary is entirely within the heuristic function itself. Higher levels add complexity without catching additional failure modes.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a list of addresses, mock the geocoding layer to return pre-set coordinates, compute the route via the TSP heuristic, and assert that the total route distance is within 1.5x of the known-optimal distance for those coordinates. Test multiple instances (e.g., TSP benchmark sets) to ensure the heuristic consistently meets the approximation ratio.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test requires only coordinate data and route computation; both are deterministic and can be fully verified at Unit level with mocked geocoding.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: Time estimation is deterministic arithmetic: time = distance / speed. Real failures would be formula bugs (wrong operation, incorrect variable used, missing road-type adjustment). These are all logic errors testable at Unit level. No I/O, external APIs, or randomness is involved. Mocking the distance matrix and passing speed values directly to the time estimation function is sufficient to catch all real failures in this calculation.
behaviors.test_description: Provide known distance and speed values (including different road types), call the time estimation function, and assert output equals distance / speed with correct road-type multiplier applied. Test boundary cases like distance=0 and multiple road types (highway, local roads, etc.).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Pure arithmetic formula verification requires no infrastructure beyond input values and output assertion.

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: The validator is a comparison and subtraction operation: if (total_time > shift_hours) reject and return (total_time - shift_hours). Real failures are logic bugs: wrong comparison operator (>= vs >), incorrect excess calculation, or off-by-one errors. These are all caught by Unit tests passing known total times and shift hours and asserting correct rejection/acceptance and excess amounts. No external dependencies or randomness.
behaviors.test_description: Provide total_time and shift_hours values; call the validator; assert that it rejects when total_time > shift_hours and returns correct excess amount. Test boundary cases: total_time == shift_hours, total_time slightly above, significantly above.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Logic test requires only scalar inputs and output verification; fully testable at Unit level.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is a logic bug: the code doesn't properly check for empty geocoding results, doesn't skip the address in route optimization, or doesn't report it. These can be caught by mocking the geocoding API to return empty results for one address and verifying the service skips it and reports it. However, there is a non-negligible risk that the real failure involves the actual geocoding API response format or network behavior (timeout, malformed response), which would only be caught at Integration or System level. Unit-level testing with a comprehensive mock is the minimum if the mock accurately captures the real API contract, but real failures related to actual API behavior might slip through.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the geocoding API to return empty results for one address in the list. Call the service with mixed valid and invalid addresses. Assert that: (1) the service skips the address with empty geocoding results, (2) the optimized route includes only the successfully geocoded addresses, (3) the skipped address is reported in the result payload.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test verifies logic correctness at Unit level, but assumes the mock accurately reflects the real API contract. A more comprehensive test at Integration level (calling the real geocoding API) would catch additional failure modes.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: Duplicate consolidation is pure data processing logic on the input list: detection of duplicates, merging of delivery items, deduplication of the address list. Real failures are logic bugs: duplicate detection failing, items not merged correctly, or addresses visited twice. These are all caught by passing an input list with duplicates and asserting the output has a single consolidated stop with merged items. No I/O, external dependencies, or randomness involved.
behaviors.test_description: Provide an input list with duplicate addresses (identical or near-identical, depending on consolidation strategy) with different delivery items. Call the service. Assert that the output route has exactly one stop per unique address and that all delivery items for that address are combined into a single stop entry.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Deduplication logic is testable purely at Unit level with input/output verification.
